description: All men are mortal. Socrates is a man. The rest is logic.

guides.text: A classic syllogism. We are given that every $x$ who is a man is mortal, and that Socrates is a man. Our goal: Socrates is mortal. This is the **Aristotelian syllogism** — the original example of logical deduction.
Start by assuming the first [premise|premise]: $\forall x.\; man(x) \to mortal(x)$.
Now assume the second [premise|premise]: Socrates is a man, `(App man socrates)`.

The rule `forall-elim` instantiates a [universal-quantifier|universal quantifier]. From $\forall x.\; man(x) \to mortal(x)$ at step 1, replace $x$ with $socrates$ to get $man(socrates) \to mortal(socrates)$. The second argument is the term to substitute — here, `socrates`.
Now you have $man(socrates) \to mortal(socrates)$ at step 3 and $man(socrates)$ at step 2. [modus-ponens|Modus ponens] gives you $mortal(socrates)$ — exactly the goal. The syllogism is complete.

unlocks.name: ∀-elim
unlocks.description: From $\forall x.\; \varphi$, derive $\varphi$ with any term $t$ substituted for $x$. What holds for everything holds for any particular thing.
